input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements, operating in the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain where resource-constrained ECUs necessitate computational offloading for safety-critical functions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: (1) Data Monitoring via a UI for real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, along with data saving and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading of the ACC system (lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance) to cloud resources; and (3) Remote Management of ACC functions including enabling/disabling, speed setting, and emergency braking commands. Target users include operators for real-time monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting using collected data.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy between physical and digital components for computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, as mandated by industry standards with hardware limitations necessitating this specific timeframe.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs a hierarchical entity-based architecture with three distinct entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) deployed on separate hardware components. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package, with the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents. The Physical Twin Entity hosts general automotive functionality such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, deviating from ISO 23247 RA's OME by consolidating hardware resources, impacting functionality ownership and engineering processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architected per ISO 23247 RA, software components are mapped to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages. Each entity (Physical, Digital, User) is deployed on dedicated hardware, with the Physical Twin Entity extending beyond the standard's OME to handle general automotive functionality. System-wide coordination relies on 100 ms time synchronization accuracy for computation offloading, and the User Interface FE is subdivided into 3D Visualization and Control Panel components within the User Entity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system implements computational models and data components per ISO 23247's reference architecture. Key elements include the ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) model as a Controlling FE for vehicle speed regulation, Data Collection for sensor input acquisition, Report Generation for diagnostic output creation, Data Storage using an SQL database for structured persistence, and Data Translation for converting raw sensor data into standardized formats. These components form an integrated data flow for real-time analysis and control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller for motor speed regulation, a DC motor for propulsion, and a steering servo for directional control. The Actuation Functional Entity converts desired speed commands into precise motor voltage signals via PWM, while a Control Panel enables remote management. Actuation ranges are constrained by hardware limits (e.g., 0-100% throttle via PWM), with safety mechanisms for overcurrent protection and emergency stop protocols.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors consist of a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera. Raw sensor data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Functional Entity to generate object tracking information.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, object tracks, projected driving path) is transmitted via UDP over Wi-Fi to the Digital Twin Entity. The Data Collecting FE ingests raw data, followed by preprocessing via the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component for LiDAR data. The Data Translation component serializes all data streams, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. ROS2 handles internal communication between Digital Twin components, while UDP over Wi-Fi manages inter-entity data flows for real-time monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (e.g., ACC, steering, motor actuation) are processed by the Controlling FE and Data Translation component, which serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Commands are communicated via ROS2 within the Digital Twin and UDP over Wi-Fi between entities to the Actuation FE for physical actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS maintains a maximum network latency of 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity. Communication protocols include ROS2 for intra-entity software components and Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. Each entity is deployed on separate hardware components, as shown in the Architecture and deployment view (Fig. 2).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Data storage is external to the vehicle per Requirement R3, with computational tasks offloaded to cloud infrastructure as specified in Section III.A. Each entity is deployed on dedicated hardware components, supporting scalable cloud-based deployment models.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin system is utilized across multiple lifecycle stages, including the design phase of automotive systems, during vehicle operation for real-time monitoring, and as a product lifecycle management tool to track and optimize the entire product lifecycle from development to end-of-life.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system supports decision-making through User Entity interfaces for monitoring data and controlling Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME). Functional Entities transform raw data into human-interpretable formats to enable actionable insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247 standard for Digital Twin framework in manufacturing, specifically Part 2 (Reference Architecture), which defines an entity-based structure including Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities (FEs) handle specific tasks such as actuation, data transformation, and user interfacing, with OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) cited as an example implementation for interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system complies with ISO 15622:2018 performance requirements for Adaptive Cruise Control systems and adheres to the latency specifications defined in 3GPP TS 22.185 for V2X services to ensure safety-critical operations through timely data exchange.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately identifies the system as a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle with lower and upper chassis layers, operating in the ACC domain. It correctly mentions the need for computational offloading due to resource-constrained ECUs. Quotes: 'developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT for a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis... autonomy elements'.

The extraction provides a detailed and accurate description of the three services: Data Monitoring (UI for visualization, saving, reports), Computation Offloading (ACC system to cloud), and Remote Management (enabling/disabling, speed setting, emergency braking). It also correctly identifies target users (operators and developers). Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... a user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports... real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system... determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', '3) Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists numerous tools and technologies used to implement the Digital Twin. Examples include 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0', programming languages 'Python 3.8' and 'C++', hardware like 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano', and the 'ISO 23247' reference architecture. Since the text contains relevant evidence that the extraction ignored, the score is 1.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the specific 100 ms requirement for clock synchronization, correctly identifies the purpose (computation offloading), and notes the context of safety-critical standards versus hardware limitations found in the source. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The description correctly details the entity-based architecture, the deployment on separate hardware, and the specific implementation using ROS2 packages. It accurately describes the split of the User Interface FE and the specific deviation from the ISO 23247 RA regarding the Physical Twin Entity and its impact. Supported by quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA', 'such deviations from the standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.'

The extraction provides a comprehensive overview of the system's orchestration, mapping components to ISO 23247 FEs, and detailing the hardware separation and specific deviations (Physical Twin Entity vs OME). It correctly integrates the time synchronization requirement for coordination. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design', 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'Physical Twin Entity... can host general automotive functionality', 'User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.'

The document describes the internal architecture and communication between the Digital Twin entities (Physical, Digital, User) but does not describe integration with external systems, interoperability with other Digital Twins, or enterprise system integration patterns. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is the correct assessment.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and well-supported by the provided documents. It correctly maps the specific software components to ISO 23247 Functional Entities as described in Table I and Section IV-B. Specifically, it identifies the ACC as the Controlling FE ('ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed. Controlling FE'), Data Collection as the Data Collecting FE ('Data Collection Collects object tracks and ego vehicle state. Data Collecting FE'), Report Generation as the Reporting FE ('Report Generation Generates human-readable reports... Reporting FE'), and Data Storage using a SQL database ('Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'). The mention of Data Translation aligns with the Cross-System Entity description ('Data Translation Translation of exchanged data between entities and FEs').

The extraction correctly identifies the physical actuators listed in Section III-B, including the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Motor', and 'Steering Servo'. It accurately describes the function of the Actuation Functional Entity based on Table I ('Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE') and the role of the Control Panel ('Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands'). However, the specific technical details regarding '0-100% throttle via PWM', 'safety mechanisms for overcurrent protection', and 'emergency stop protocols' are not explicitly mentioned in the provided source text. While the 'emergency braking' command is mentioned as a UI feature, specific hardware safety protocols are not described.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific sensors listed in the source: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'. It also accurately identifies the processing component, 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking', and its role as a 'Data Pre-Processing FE'. The description is specific and well-supported by the evidence.

While the document mentions general concepts like 'high-fidelity' and defines requirements for 'network latency' and 'clock synchronization' (R7, R8), it does not describe validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, error detection and correction mechanisms, or specific quality assurance processes for the models and data as defined by the characteristic. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is a correct assessment.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and correctly identifies the data types (e.g., 'LiDAR point clouds', 'vehicle state', 'object tracks'), the preprocessing steps ('Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking'), and the specific roles of the 'Data Collecting FE' and 'Data Translation' component, matching the text: 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'. It also correctly identifies ROS2 for internal communication. However, the claim that data is transmitted via 'UDP' is not fully supported by the provided text, which cuts off at 'using U' regarding the Wi-Fi protocol.

Accurately describes the flow of control commands from the 'Controlling FE' and 'Data Translation' to the 'Actuation FE'. The description of the Data Translation component ('serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions') is directly supported. The use of ROS2 for internal communication is also supported. Like the previous characteristic, it includes 'UDP' for the Wi-Fi protocol, which is not visible in the truncated source text ('using U'), but otherwise provides strong technical detail grounded in the sources.

The extraction is highly specific and directly grounded in the requirements and architecture section. It correctly cites the 100 ms latency requirement (R7), distinguishes between intra-entity (ROS2) and inter-entity (Wi-Fi) protocols, and notes the deployment on separate hardware components.

The description accurately reflects the storage requirements (R3) and the computational offloading strategy described in Section III.A ('allocating compute to better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud'). It also correctly identifies the separate hardware deployment of entities. The mention of 'scalable' is a reasonable inference from the cloud-based fleet coordination context, though the specific word 'scalable' is not explicitly attached to the deployment model in the text.

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_5_judge.reasoning: Identifies the correct lifecycle stages (design, operation, product lifecycle management) where Digital Twins are utilized in the automotive context, as described in the background section. However, it lacks specific technical details present in the text regarding the activities within these stages, such as 'cyber-physical experimentation facilities' or specific 'health monitoring' examples. Supported by quotes: 'During the design phase of automotive systems, DTs are often used as cyber-physical experimentation facilities', 'During vehicle operation, DTs are used for a variety of optimization and health monitoring purposes', 'DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management tool in support of digital threads of vehicles'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. While the document describes the architectural design and component mapping (Section IV), it does not provide details on quality assurance practices, specific testing and validation approaches, version control, or evolution management strategies, which are the core requirements of this characteristic.

The extraction accurately reflects the role of the User Entity and Functional Entities in monitoring and decision support. Quotes: 'User Entity provides interfaces for users to interact with the Digital Twin Entity, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME', 'Functional Entities (FEs)... responsible for... transforming data into a human-interpretable format'.

The extraction is highly specific and accurate, reflecting the detailed explanation of ISO 23247 in the text. It correctly identifies Part 2 (Reference Architecture), the entity-based structure (OME, Data Collection, DT Entity, User Entity), the role of Functional Entities (actuation, data transformation, user interfacing), and cites OPC UA as an implementation example.
